label: Define core terms/equations on first use; equations get their own scene; visible throughline; plain-not-poetic; bulleted enumerations

changelog.summary:
Refinements within the v4 methodology, from reader feedback on the v4.0.0 solar-thermal explainer (portal-v4). (1) Just-in-time prerequisites must actually DEFINE the term: a named quantity the arc runs on (Δv, specific impulse) or a governing equation gets a plain one-line gloss the first time it appears — never compute with a symbol the reader wasn't handed in words. (2) A governing equation is its own scene: present it as a labeled equation diagram (every symbol, unit, meaning) with the interactive chart that explores it as a separate following scene — never crammed into a chart corner. (3) The throughline must be visible, not just intended: every caption opens by connecting to the prior step, and part ledes explicitly hand off; the coherence reviewer fails ledes/captions that reference a missing or non-adjacent scene or lack an explicit hand-off. (4) Plain, not poetic: banned lyrical antithesis and magazine-feature flourish where a plain declarative belongs (added as a named slop tell with the failing example). (5) Enumerations render as bullets: 3+ parallel items in a caption or lede become a real list (renderRichText gained minimal '- ' list support; caption/lede wrappers moved from

to

).

Methodology reframe from studying the best prior art (Ciechanowski, Distill's 'Communicating with Interactive Articles', moonshine, 3Blue1Brown). The scriptwriter prompt was rewritten from an accreted list of negative rules into a coherent positive spec: (1) story-discovery first — commit to ONE insight and ONE concrete running example (a named benchmark/device/scenario) before any scene; (2) one connected narrative throughline — every scene returns to and advances the same running example instead of inventing fresh toys, fewer/deeper scenes over exhaustive coverage; (3) teach prerequisites just-in-time and briefly instead of front-loading a thick refresher block; (4) guided discovery — predict-before-reveal moments; (5) details-on-demand — overview first, secondary detail in labels; (6) selective interactivity — a clean labeled diagram or a single sentence is allowed where a slider wouldn't earn its place (over-interactivizing is a known failure). All hard-won specifics kept but subordinated to the throughline: caption math \(...\), no undefined terms, idiomatic-only metaphors, 2-3 sentence abstract, introduce canonical examples by name/role, show the cause, establish baselines. The prose reviewer was expanded into a whole-explainer coherence reviewer (reads title+abstract+hook+arc+scenes end to end) that fails: no single insight, no running example, broken throughline, reader-gets-lost, and filler scenes.

From Madrona v3.2.0 chat feedback: introduce canonical examples/benchmarks (cartpole, hide-and-seek) by name and role before using them; establish the field's default/'naive' baseline before critiquing it; make the cause of an effect triggerable in the interactive itself, not just asserted. Reviewer fails scenes that invoke an un-introduced example/benchmark or baseline.

From Madrona reader feedback: a prerequisite-gap ramp gives a refresher scene to every prerequisite the paper needs that the audience doesn't already know (bedrock nouns of the paper's field — agent, simulator, GPU/warp — count as undefined terms for an out-of-field reader). Scenes must show the cause of an effect, not just the effect, and make their single takeaway unmistakable; the reviewer now fails scenes whose takeaway is unclear or that show an effect without its cause.
List abstract tightened to 2-3 short sentences (down from 2-4), enforced by a deterministic sentence-count cap in the prose reviewer. The explainer-top hook stays a separate, motivational lead-in.

Clear-writing rules from plain-language research: the abstract is 2-4 short sentences (one idea each, ~15-20 words, headline number on its own line), never a run-on. The prose reviewer now also evaluates the abstract, and a deterministic check flags any sentence over 30 words across the abstract, hook, and captions.

The list gist (oneSentenceClaim) must read like a plain-language abstract, not a dense claim. The hook must be sensible English with no slop, and any metaphor must be genuinely idiomatic (no invented images like 'a fort out of movable boxes'). Added a prose reviewer that reads the hook + captions before graphics and auto-revises slop / non-idiomatic metaphors / undefined terms; the scene reviewer also fails caption slop.

Visuals carry the explanation: every scene is a labeled diagram (structure) or an interactive chart with the independent variable on a reader-moved control (relationships). Captions cut from 3-5 sentences to 1-2 that point at the visual. Metaphor is a doorway, not a residence (introduce, then name the real term and drop the metaphor). Removed the story-roles rule.

Feynman-first four-part arc (building blocks -> new tech -> why it beats existing -> why now). 3-5 sentence captions in Feynman voice with Zinsser economy. Introduce-then-use vocabulary; sustained metaphors with explicit mappings; story roles over algebra labels; honest black boxes for beyond-audience mechanisms.
